original_abstract:
Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are a major environmental concern due to their persistent toxicity and well-documented carcinogenic risks. Because they are semi-volatile, PAHs partition between the vapor and particulate phases in the atmosphere, where they undergo complex chemical transformations. This article provides a comprehensive review of current knowledge regarding PAH deposition, vapor–particle partitioning, and atmospheric reactivity. 

PAHs react with several key atmospheric oxidants, including the hydroxyl (OH) radical, ozone (O₃), the nitrate radical (NO₃), and nitrogen dioxide (NO₂). We examine rate coefficient data for the gas-phase reactions of lower molecular weight PAHs as well as the heterogeneous reactions of heavier, particle-bound compounds. While data for 2–3 ring vapor-phase PAHs are relatively robust and consistent, significant gaps remains for 4-ring compounds. Furthermore, experimental data for heterogeneous reactions involving five or more rings are less comprehensive and vary significantly depending on the chemical nature of the substrate and the localized reaction conditions. 

The atmospheric degradation of PAHs typically leads to the formation of oxygenated and nitrated derivatives, which are often more toxic than their parent compounds. Finally, this review explores the potential for PAHs to undergo long-range transport and assesses the findings of recent numerical modeling studies. We conclude by identifying critical research priorities necessary to better understand the global fate of these pollutants.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract Abstract Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) have raised a great deal of environmental health concern due to their persistence, toxicities and well established carcinogenic potential. As semi-volatile chemicals, PAHs exist in all phases of the environment and can change among gas, aerosol and solid phases or species depending on ambient conditions. This review paper summarises current understanding of PAH deposition, vapour-to-particle partitioning and atmospheric reactivity.

PAHs react with a number of important atmospheric oxidants including the hydroxyl (OH) radical, ozone (O3), the nitrate radical (NO3) and nitrogen dioxide (NO2). We summarize rate coefficient data for the gas-phase reactions of lower molecular weight PAHs and heterogeneous reactions of the heavier, condensed-phase species. While there is relatively little uncertainty in the rate coefficients for the gas-phase reactions of 2–3 ring PAHs, there is considerable uncertainty in those for the 4-ring species and experimental data for heterogeneous reactions of the five and more ring species are limited and very dependent on the composition of the reactive surface and the local environment.

PAHs are subject to considerable abiotic degradation within the atmosphere. The main products are derived through oxidation and/or nitration reactions, resulting in species generally considered to be more toxic than the parent compound. This review also discusses the potential for long-range transport of PAHs and summarises a range of numerical modelling studies that have investigated the global dispersion of these compounds. Finally, research needs must be identified to elucidate the global fate and behaviour of these pollutants.
